2605.12452.title: The Algorithmic Caricature: Auditing LLM-Generated Political Discourse Across Crisis Events
2605.12452.paper_summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate fluent political text at scale, raising concerns about synthetic discourse during crises and social conflict. Existing AI-text detection often focuses on sentence-level cues such as perplexity, burstiness, or token irregularities, but these signals may weaken as generative systems improve. We instead adopt a Computational Social Science perspective and ask whether synthetic political discourse behaves like an observed online population. We construct a paired corpus of 1,789,406 posts across nine crisis events: COVID-19, the Jan. 6 Capitol attack, the 2020 and 2024 U.S. elections, Dobbs/Roe v. Wade, the 2020 BLM protests, U.S. midterms, the Utah shooting, and the U.S.-Iran war. For each event, we compare observed discourse from social platforms with synthetic discourse generated for the same context. We evaluate four dimensions: emotional intensity, structural regularity, lexical-ideological framing, and cross-event dependency, using mean gaps and dispersion evidence. Across events, synthetic discourse is fluent but population-level unrealistic. It is generally more negative and less dispersed in sentiment, structurally more regular, and lexically more abstract than observed discourse. Observed discourse instead shows broader emotional variation, longer-tailed structural distributions, and more context-specific, colloquial lexical markers. These differences are event-dependent: larger for fast-moving, decentralized crises and smaller for formal or institutionally mediated events. We summarize them with a simple event-level measure, the Caricature Gap. Our findings suggest that the main limitation of synthetic political discourse is not grammar or fluency, but reduced population realism. Population-level auditing complements traditional text-detection and provides a CSS framework for evaluating the social realism of generated discourse.
2605.12452.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）可以大規模生成流暢的政治文本，這引發了對於危機和社會衝突期間合成話語的擔憂。現有的 AI 文本檢測通常專注於句子級別的線索，例如困惑度、突發性或標記不規則性，但隨著生成系統的改進，這些信號可能會減弱。我們則採用計算社會科學的視角，詢問合成政治話語是否像觀察到的在線人群一樣行為。我們構建了一個包含 1,789,406 條帖子配對的語料庫，涵蓋九個危機事件：COVID-19、1 月 6 日國會襲擊、2020 年和 2024 年美國大選、Dobbs/Roe v. Wade、2020 年 BLM 抗議、美國中期選舉、猶他州槍擊事件和美國-伊朗戰爭。對於每個事件，我們將社交平台上觀察到的話語與為相同背景生成的合成話語進行比較。我們評估四個維度：情感強度、結構規則性、詞彙-意識形態框架和跨事件依賴性，使用平均差距和分散證據。在各事件中，合成話語流暢但在人口層面上不現實。它通常情感上更負面且分散性較低，結構上更規則，詞彙上比觀察到的話語更抽象。觀察到的話語則顯示出更廣泛的情感變化、更長尾的結構分佈，以及更多上下文特定的口語詞彙標記。這些差異是事件依賴的：在快速變動的去中心化危機中更大，而在正式或機構中介的事件中則較小。我們用一個簡單的事件級別指標，即漫畫差距，來總結它們。我們的發現表明，合成政治話語的主要限制不在於語法或流暢性，而在於降低的人口現實性。人口層面的審核補充了傳統的文本檢測，並提供了一個 CSS 框架來評估生成話語的社會現實性。
2605.12452.author: Gunjan et.al.
